generator client {
  provider = "prisma-client-js"
  binaryTargets = ["native", "debian-openssl-3.0.x"]
  engineType = "client"

}

datasource db {
  provider = "postgresql"
}

enum Role {
  CUSTOMER
  ADMIN
}

enum BookingType {
  LAUNCH
  RETRIEVAL
}

enum BookingStatus {
  PENDING
  COMPLETED
  CANCELLED
}

model User {
  id           String   @id @default(cuid())
  name         String
  username     String?  @unique
  email        String   @unique
  phone        String?
  address      String?
  passwordHash String
  role           Role      @default(CUSTOMER)
  suspendedUntil DateTime?
  createdAt      DateTime  @default(now())
  updatedAt      DateTime  @updatedAt

  vessels              Vessel[]
  dailyMessagesUpdated DailyMessage[] @relation("DailyMessageUpdatedBy")
  manualBookings       Booking[]      @relation("ManualBookingCreatedBy")
  passwordResets       PasswordReset[]
}

model Vessel {
  id         String   @id @default(cuid())
  userId     String
  boatName   String
  boatType   String?
  boatNumber String
  yardNumber String?  @unique
  createdAt  DateTime @default(now())
  updatedAt  DateTime @updatedAt

  owner    User      @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  bookings Booking[]

  @@index([userId])
  @@index([yardNumber])
}

model Booking {
  id              String        @id @default(cuid())
  vesselId        String
  type            BookingType
  serviceDate     DateTime
  requestedAt     DateTime      @default(now())
  status          BookingStatus @default(PENDING)
  completedAt     DateTime?
  stayingOnMooring Boolean      @default(false)
  note            String?
  createdByUserId String?

  vessel      Vessel @relation(fields: [vesselId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  createdBy   User?  @relation("ManualBookingCreatedBy", fields: [createdByUserId], references: [id], onDelete: SetNull)

  @@index([type, serviceDate, status, requestedAt])
  @@index([vesselId, type, serviceDate])
}

model PasswordReset {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  userId    String
  token     String   @unique
  method    String   // "email" | "phone"
  used      Boolean  @default(false)
  expiresAt DateTime
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())

  user User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@index([token])
  @@index([userId])
}

model DailyMessage {
  id               String   @id @default(cuid())
  serviceDate      DateTime @unique
  message          String
  launchStartTime  String?
  retrievalStartTime String?
  updatedAt        DateTime @updatedAt
  updatedByUserId  String?

  updatedBy User? @relation("DailyMessageUpdatedBy", fields: [updatedByUserId], references: [id], onDelete: SetNull)

  @@index([serviceDate])
}

input.content:
==============================================================================
WESTCOAST MARINE PARKS — RENDER DEPLOYMENT GUIDE
==============================================================================

WHY IT ISN'T PLUG-AND-PLAY
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This app has three characteristics that break the defaults on every platform:

  1. Socket.io (real-time WebSockets)
  2. Prisma with a driver adapter instead of the standard schema URL
  3. Missing or misconfigured environment variables at deploy time

Each platform hit a different combination of these.


==============================================================================
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS — WHY VERCEL AND NETLIFY FAILED
==============================================================================

PROBLEM 1: Socket.io is incompatible with serverless platforms
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Vercel and Netlify are SERVERLESS. Every API request is a short-lived Lambda
function that starts, handles the request, and is immediately destroyed.

Your socket setup in src/pages/api/socket.ts does this:
  anyRes.socket.server.io = io;
  globalThis.__io = io;

This works by attaching Socket.io to the underlying Node.js HTTP server — a
server that must stay ALIVE for WebSocket connections to persist. On Vercel/
Netlify there is no persistent server. The function dies after the request.
WebSocket upgrades never happen. Even if they did, the connection would drop
the moment the next function instance spins up.

This is also why your realtime.ts emitQueuesUpdated() silently does nothing
on those platforms — globalThis.__io is always undefined in serverless.

PROBLEM 2: DATABASE_URL was empty on Vercel
───────────────────────────────────────────
Checking .vercel/.env.production.local, the production value was:
  DATABASE_URL=""

An empty string. Prisma connected to nothing, so every API call that touches
the database would 500. This was a configuration error, not a code error —
the env var was never set in the Vercel dashboard.

PROBLEM 3: JWT_SECRET had a trailing newline on Vercel
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
In .vercel/.env.production.local:
  JWT_SECRET="46a5190...3092ce\n"

The \n was literal — it became part of the secret. Tokens signed locally
would fail verification in production because the key was different.

PROBLEM 4: Netlify's serverless handler wraps next start
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
@netlify/plugin-nextjs (which is in your devDependencies) rewrites the app
into Netlify Functions. Socket.io's server-attach pattern does not survive
that wrapper. This is the same root cause as Problem 1, just a different
implementation.


==============================================================================
WHY RENDER WORKS (AND IS THE RIGHT CHOICE)
==============================================================================

Render Web Services run a PERSISTENT Node.js process. `npm start` keeps the
server alive. Socket.io attaches to that server on the first request to
/api/socket and stays attached. WebSocket connections remain open. The
globalThis.__io reference lives for the lifetime of the process.

This matches exactly how the app runs locally with `npm run dev`.


==============================================================================
RENDER DEPLOYMENT STEPS
==============================================================================

STEP 1 — Create a Web Service on Render
─────────────────────────────────────────
  - Dashboard → New → Web Service
  - Connect your GitHub repo
  - Name: westcoast-marine-parks (or whatever you prefer)
  - Region: Oregon (US West) — closest to your users in BC
  - Branch: main

STEP 2 — Set the build and start commands
───────────────────────────────────────────
  Build Command:  npm install && npm run build
  Start Command:  npm start

  The `postinstall` script already runs `prisma generate` automatically
  during `npm install`, so you do NOT need to add it manually.

STEP 3 — Set environment variables in Render Dashboard
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Go to your service → Environment → Add Environment Variables

  REQUIRED:
  ┌─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Variable            │ Value                                            │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ DATABASE_URL        │ Your full Neon connection string (see note below)│
  │ JWT_SECRET          │ A random 64-char hex string (no trailing newline)│
  │ NODE_ENV            │ production                                       │
  │ NEXT_PUBLIC_BASE_URL│ https://your-app-name.onrender.com               │
  └─────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  OPTIONAL (needed for password reset emails):
  ┌─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ SMTP_HOST           │ Your SMTP server host                            │
  │ SMTP_PORT           │ 587                                              │
  │ SMTP_USER           │ Your SMTP username                               │
  │ SMTP_PASS           │ Your SMTP password                               │
  │ SMTP_FROM           │ noreply@yourdomain.com                           │
  └─────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  OPTIONAL (for tide data):
  ┌─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ WORLDTIDES_API_KEY  │ Your WorldTides API key                          │
  └─────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

DATABASE_URL NOTE:
  Your Neon connection string is in .vercel/.env.production.local (the one
  from the Netlify .env file, not the empty Vercel one):
    postgresql://neondb_owner:npg_aTjiL4p6PBWF@ep-damp-morning-...neon.tech/...

  Use the POOLER URL (the one with -pooler in the hostname) for production.
  Add ?sslmode=require at the end if not already present.

JWT_SECRET NOTE:
  Generate a clean secret with no trailing characters:
    node -e "console.log(require('crypto').randomBytes(32).toString('hex'))"
  Paste ONLY the hex string into Render — no quotes, no newline.

STEP 4 — Set the Node version
───────────────────────────────
  Your package.json does not specify an engines field. Render defaults to
  Node 18 which works. To pin it explicitly, add to package.json:
    "engines": { "node": ">=20" }

STEP 5 — Deploy
────────────────
  Click "Create Web Service". Render will:
    1. Clone the repo
    2. Run npm install (this runs prisma generate via postinstall)
    3. Run npm run build (next build)
    4. Run npm start (next start — persistent server)

STEP 6 — Run the database migration
─────────────────────────────────────
  After the first deploy, open Render Shell or run locally with the
  production DATABASE_URL:
    npx prisma migrate deploy

  Do NOT use `prisma migrate dev` in production. Use `migrate deploy`.

STEP 7 — Seed the database (optional)
───────────────────────────────────────
  If you want the dev seed data (admin + customer accounts):
    npm run db:seed
  Run this from the Render Shell or locally pointing at the production DB.


==============================================================================
KNOWN ISSUES IN THE CODEBASE (not blocking but worth fixing)
==============================================================================

1. MIDDLEWARE DEPRECATION WARNING
   File: src/middleware.ts
   Next.js 16 renamed the middleware convention to "proxy". The build warns:
     "The middleware file convention is deprecated. Please use proxy instead."
   The file still works now but will break in a future Next.js version.
   Fix: rename src/middleware.ts → src/proxy.ts (and update the export name).

2. PRISMA SCHEMA MISSING url IN DATASOURCE
   File: prisma/schema.prisma
   The datasource block has no url field:
     datasource db {
       provider = "postgresql"
     }
   This relies on prisma.config.ts (Prisma 7's new config API) to inject the
   URL. The runtime db.ts bypasses this entirely by passing DATABASE_URL
   directly to PrismaPg adapter. This works but is unconventional and will
   confuse anyone debugging Prisma CLI commands (migrate, studio, etc.) if
   DATABASE_URL is not set in their shell.

3. DUPLICATE DB CLIENT FILE
   Files: src/lib/db.ts and src/lib/db1.ts
   db1.ts imports `dotenv/config` which is not in package.json. If db1.ts
   is ever imported it will throw a module not found error at runtime.
   db1.ts appears to be an old draft — delete it.

4. IN-MEMORY RATE LIMITER
   File: src/lib/rate-limit.ts
   The rate limiter uses a Map stored in process memory. It resets on every
   Render deploy or restart, and if you ever scale to 2+ instances the
   counts will not be shared between them. This is noted in the source code.
   Acceptable for a single-instance deploy; replace with Redis (Upstash) if
   you scale horizontally.

5. @netlify/plugin-nextjs IN devDependencies
   This is installed on every build including Render's. It adds build time
   and is completely unused on Render. Remove it:
     npm uninstall @netlify/plugin-nextjs
   Also delete netlify.toml.


==============================================================================
SUMMARY OF CHANGES NEEDED BEFORE DEPLOYING TO RENDER
==============================================================================

  [ ] Set DATABASE_URL in Render environment variables (non-empty, pooler URL)
  [ ] Set JWT_SECRET in Render environment variables (clean hex, no newline)
  [ ] Set NODE_ENV=production
  [ ] Set NEXT_PUBLIC_BASE_URL to your Render domain
  [ ] Run prisma migrate deploy after first deploy
  [ ] (Optional) Remove db1.ts
  [ ] (Optional) Remove @netlify/plugin-nextjs and netlify.toml


==============================================================================
RENDER SERVICE HEALTH CHECK
==============================================================================

Render pings your service to verify it's up. Next.js does not expose /health
by default. Render will use / (the homepage) for health checks which is fine.
If you want a dedicated endpoint, add:
  src/app/api/health/route.ts
  export async function GET() { return Response.json({ status: "ok" }) }
And set the health check path to /api/health in the Render dashboard.

==============================================================================

content.text:
Here's the full diagnosis:

---

## Why it's not plug-and-play — 3 root causes

### 1. Socket.io kills Vercel and Netlify instantly
These platforms are **serverless** — each API request spawns a Lambda that lives for milliseconds. Your `/api/socket.ts` works by attaching Socket.io to a running Node.js HTTP server (`res.socket.server.io = io`). That server simply doesn't exist on serverless runtimes. WebSocket upgrades never happen, and `globalThis.__io` is always `undefined`, so `emitQueuesUpdated()` silently does nothing. **Render runs a persistent process** — `npm start` keeps the server alive — which is exactly what Socket.io needs. This is the primary reason Render is correct and Vercel/Netlify aren't.

### 2. DATABASE_URL was empty on Vercel
Looking inside `.vercel/.env.production.local`, the production value was literally `DATABASE_URL=""`. Every DB call 500'd. This was a configuration mistake, not a code error.

### 3. JWT_SECRET had a literal `\n` embedded on Vercel
The Vercel env file shows `JWT_SECRET="...3092ce\n"` — the newline became part of the key. Tokens signed locally wouldn't verify in production.

---

## What to do on Render

The document at `RENDER_DEPLOYMENT.txt` has the full step-by-step, but the critical checklist:

| What | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Build command** | `npm install && npm run build` |
| **Start command** | `npm start` |
| **`DATABASE_URL`** | Use the Neon pooler URL from your `.env.local` |
| **`JWT_SECRET`** | Fresh hex string, `node -e "console.log(require('crypto').randomBytes(32).toString('hex'))"` — no trailing newline |
| **`NODE_ENV`** | `production` |
| **`NEXT_PUBLIC_BASE_URL`** | Your `.onrender.com` domain |
| **After first deploy** | Run `npx prisma migrate deploy` |

---

## Codebase issues to clean up

- **`src/lib/db1.ts`** — dead file that imports `dotenv/config` (not in package.json). Delete it.
- **`src/middleware.ts`** — Next.js 16 deprecated this in favour of `proxy.ts`. Still works now but needs renaming eventually.
- **`datasource db`** in schema.prisma has no `url` field — relies on `prisma.config.ts` (Prisma 7 feature). Works, but makes `prisma studio` and `prisma migrate` confusing without `DATABASE_URL` in your shell.
- **`@netlify/plugin-nextjs` in devDependencies** — installs on every Render build unnecessarily. Remove it.

input.prompt:
You are making pre-deployment code fixes to a Next.js 15/16 app at /home/nugad/westcoast-marine-parks. Make every change listed below, run `npm run build` to verify, then commit everything in a single conventional commit. Do not push.

## Fixes to make

### 1. Delete src/lib/db1.ts
This file is an unused draft that imports `dotenv/config` (not in package.json). Just delete it.

### 2. Rename middleware → proxy (Next.js 16 convention)
Next.js 16 deprecated `src/middleware.ts` in favour of `src/proxy.ts`. The build warns about this on every build.
- Read src/middleware.ts first
- Create src/proxy.ts with the same content BUT rename the exported function from `middleware` to `proxy` (Next.js 16 expects a `proxy` export in proxy.ts)
- Also update the config export's matcher — keep it identical
- Delete src/middleware.ts

### 3. Fix prisma/schema.prisma datasource — add url field
The datasource block currently has NO url field:
  datasource db {
    provider = "postgresql"
  }
Add the url line so it becomes:
  datasource db {
    provider = "postgresql"
    url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
  }
This makes prisma migrate, prisma studio, and prisma db push all work correctly without needing prisma.config.ts to be loaded.

### 4. Clean up package.json
- Remove `@netlify/plugin-nextjs` from devDependencies (it's listed but we're deploying to Render, not Netlify)
- Add an `"engines"` field at the top level: `"engines": { "node": ">=20" }`
- Run `npm install` after this to update package-lock.json

### 5. Delete netlify.toml
This file is no longer needed.

### 6. Create render.yaml at the project root
This is the Render Blueprint/IaC file. Use this exact content:

```yaml
services:
  - type: web
    name: westcoast-marine-parks
    runtime: node
    buildCommand: npm install && npm run build
    startCommand: npm start
    healthCheckPath: /api/health
    envVars:
      - key: NODE_ENV
        value: production
      - key: NEXT_PUBLIC_BASE_URL
        value: https://westcoast-marine-parks.onrender.com

databases:
  - name: westcoast-marine-parks-db
    databaseName: westcoast_marine_parks
    plan: free
    region: oregon
```

### 7. Create src/app/api/health/route.ts
Render uses this for health checks:
```ts
export const runtime = "nodejs";

export async function GET() {
  return Response.json({ status: "ok" });
}
```

## After all changes
1. Run `npm run build` and confirm it succeeds with no errors (warnings are OK)
2. Commit with message: `chore: fix codebase for render deployment`
   Include co-author: Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Report back:
- Which files were changed/deleted
- Whether the build succeeded
- The git commit hash

input.description: Render API setup and env vars
input.prompt:
You are setting up a Render deployment for a Next.js app called "westcoast-marine-parks". The service already exists on Render. Your job is to:

1. Find the service ID
2. Create a new Render PostgreSQL database (free tier)
3. Set all required environment variables on the service
4. Trigger a manual deploy

## Credentials
Render API Key: rnd_zbqc6TYjt1AKsuUC7abNCUNaPgUN
Render API Base: https://api.render.com/v1
Auth header for all requests: `Authorization: Bearer rnd_zbqc6TYjt1AKsuUC7abNCUNaPgUN`
Always include: `Accept: application/json` and `Content-Type: application/json`

## Step 1 — Find the service ID
GET https://api.render.com/v1/services?limit=20
Look for the service named "westcoast-marine-parks". Extract its `id` (starts with `srv-`).

## Step 2 — Create a PostgreSQL database
Check if a postgres DB already exists first:
GET https://api.render.com/v1/postgres?limit=10

If none named "westcoast-marine-parks-db" exists, create one:
POST https://api.render.com/v1/postgres
Body:
{
  "name": "westcoast-marine-parks-db",
  "databaseName": "westcoast_marine_parks",
  "databaseUser": "wcm_user",
  "plan": "free",
  "region": "oregon",
  "version": "16"
}

After creation, poll GET https://api.render.com/v1/postgres/{postgresId} until `status` is "available" (poll every 10 seconds, max 5 minutes). Then extract the `connectionInfo.internalConnectionString` value — this is the DATABASE_URL.

If the database creation is taking too long, use the `externalConnectionString` instead.

## Step 3 — Set environment variables on the service
PUT https://api.render.com/v1/services/{serviceId}/env-vars

This is a REPLACE operation — it sets ALL env vars at once. Use this exact body (replace DATABASE_URL_VALUE with the actual connection string from step 2):

{
  "envVars": [
    { "key": "DATABASE_URL", "value": "DATABASE_URL_VALUE" },
    { "key": "JWT_SECRET", "value": "2024b445fd44e8a114351ac1a680e3832a7f22e1b38d3af23058da7b276bcadf" },
    { "key": "NODE_ENV", "value": "production" },
    { "key": "NEXT_PUBLIC_BASE_URL", "value": "https://westcoast-marine-parks.onrender.com" },
    { "key": "SMTP_HOST", "value": "mail.wcmlaunching.co.uk" },
    { "key": "SMTP_PORT", "value": "465" },
    { "key": "SMTP_USER", "value": "noreply@wcmlaunching.co.uk" },
    { "key": "SMTP_PASS", "value": "Milo3121!:)?" },
    { "key": "SMTP_FROM", "value": "noreply@wcmlaunching.co.uk" }
  ]
}

## Step 4 — Trigger a deploy
POST https://api.render.com/v1/services/{serviceId}/deploys
Body: { "clearCache": false }

## Report back
Return:
- Service ID found
- PostgreSQL database ID and status
- The DATABASE_URL connection string (internal)
- Whether env vars were set successfully (HTTP 200/201)
- Deploy ID triggered
- Any errors encountered with the raw API response

If the Render API structure is different from what's described (e.g. different field names), adapt based on the actual response and complete the task.

|---|---|

Everything is falling into place. Let me give you the full status.

---

## Deployment Status

**Two deploys are running on Render from commit `9c7078d` (the fix commit):**

| Deploy | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `dep-d799peslgb9s73d9c230` | `build_in_progress` | Auto-triggered by git push — old build cmd (no migration step) |
| `dep-d799q6dgp6ms73dfm0v0` | `queued` | Manually triggered after build cmd update — **this is the one that matters** |

The queued deploy will run: `npm install && npx prisma migrate deploy && npm run build` — it will create all tables on the fresh Render PostgreSQL before building.

**Monitor here:** https://dashboard.render.com/web/srv-d797f4vkijhs738puh70

---

## What was done end-to-end

**Code fixes (committed `9c7078d`, pushed to main):**
- Deleted `src/lib/db1.ts` (orphaned file importing non-existent `dotenv`)
- Renamed `middleware.ts` → `proxy.ts`, export renamed to `proxy` (Next.js 16)

- Added `engines: { node: ">=20" }` to `package.json`

- Build command updated to include `prisma migrate deploy`

The Render **free PostgreSQL expires 2026-05-05** (90-day free tier limit). Before then you'll need to upgrade to a paid plan or export and re-import your data. Render will email you a warning before it expires.
